questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: In which form do the water molecules have less kinetic energy?
questions.optionD: All of them have equal kinetic energy
questions.solution: Kinetic energy of particles is directly proportional to temperature. Ice at 0°C has the lowest temperature among the three states, so its molecules have the least kinetic energy. Common mistake: confusing kinetic energy with potential energy — ice has higher intermolecular potential energy (stored), but lower kinetic energy.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of these choices is defined as 'Standard Pressure'?
questions.solution: Standard pressure is defined as 1 atm = 760 mmHg (torr) = 101.325 kPa = 14.7 psi. All three options represent the same standard atmospheric pressure in different units. Common mistake: students often remember only 1 atm and forget that 760 torr and 14.7 psi are equivalent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The process of evaporation causes:
questions.solution: Evaporation is an endothermic process — the liquid absorbs heat energy from its surroundings to convert into vapour. This absorption of heat causes cooling of the surroundings. Common mistake: confusing evaporation (surface phenomenon, causes cooling) with boiling (bulk phenomenon).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Ice floats on the surface of water because:
questions.optionB: The density of both water and ice is the same
questions.solution: Density of ice (\(\approx 0.917 \text{ g/cm}^3\)) is less than density of water (\(\approx 1 \text{ g/cm}^3\)) due to the open hexagonal lattice structure of ice. Since ice is less dense, it floats. Common mistake: saying density is same for both.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following statements is **not** correct?
questions.optionB: Inter-particle spaces are maximum in the gaseous state of a substance.
questions.optionC: Particles which constitute the matter follow a zigzag path.
questions.optionD: Solid state is the most compact state of a substance.
questions.solution: According to modern atomic theory, matter is **discontinuous** (particulate) in nature — it is made up of tiny discrete particles with spaces between them. Options B, C, and D are all correct statements about matter. Common mistake: students may confuse the historical continuous model with the modern particulate model.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following has the highest kinetic energy?
questions.solution: Steam at \(100^{\circ}C\) has the highest kinetic energy because it is in the gaseous state at the highest temperature. At the same temperature (e.g., \(100^{\circ}C\)), steam has more energy than water due to the additional latent heat absorbed. Common mistake: comparing water at \(100^{\circ}C\) with steam at \(100^{\circ}C\) — steam has absorbed extra latent heat of vaporisation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Kinetic energy of molecules is directly proportional to:
questions.solution: The kinetic theory of gases states that the average kinetic energy of gas molecules is directly proportional to absolute temperature: \(KE \propto T\) (in Kelvin). It is not directly dependent on pressure. Common mistake: confusing pressure-volume relationship (Boyle's Law) with kinetic energy.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The inter-particle forces are the strongest in:
questions.solution: Sodium chloride (NaCl) is an ionic compound held together by strong electrostatic (ionic) forces between \(Na^+\) and \(Cl^-\) ions, which are much stronger than hydrogen bonds in water or van der Waals forces in hydrogen or methanol. Common mistake: choosing water because it has strong hydrogen bonding, but ionic bonds > hydrogen bonds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The substance having the maximum tendency to flow is:
questions.solution: Chlorine is a gas at room temperature. Gases have the maximum tendency to flow (highest fluidity) among all states of matter because intermolecular forces are negligible and particles move freely. Common mistake: choosing water (liquid) over chlorine (gas), not recognising that gases flow more freely than liquids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The electric bulb on long use forms a black coating on its inner surface. The process associated with this is:
questions.solution: Tungsten filament at very high temperatures undergoes sublimation — it directly converts from solid to vapour without melting. The tungsten vapour then deposits (reverse sublimation) on the cooler inner glass surface forming a black coating. Common mistake: choosing oxidation, but the bulb is filled with inert gas to prevent oxidation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subtopic: Effect of Impurities on Boiling Point
questions.question: If a few spoons of salt are dissolved in pure water then:
questions.optionC: Its freezing point becomes more than \(0^{\circ}C\)
questions.solution: Addition of a non-volatile solute (salt) to water causes elevation of boiling point (colligative property): \(\Delta T_b = K_b \times m\). The boiling point rises above \(100^{\circ}C\). Similarly, the freezing point is depressed below \(0^{\circ}C\). Common mistake: choosing option C — freezing point is depressed (lowered), not elevated.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A gas can be best liquefied:
questions.optionC: By increasing the pressure and reducing the temperature
questions.solution: Liquefaction of gas requires reducing the kinetic energy (by lowering temperature) and bringing molecules closer together (by increasing pressure). Both conditions together are most effective. Common mistake: thinking only pressure or only temperature change is sufficient for liquefaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is **not** correct regarding gases?
questions.optionC: Gases have weak tendency to diffuse.
questions.optionD: Gases have weak intermolecular forces of attraction.
questions.solution: Gases have the **highest** tendency to diffuse among all states of matter because of large intermolecular spaces and high kinetic energy of particles. Options A, B, and D are all correct properties of gases. Common mistake: confusing diffusion tendency — gases diffuse fastest, liquids slower, solids slowest.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The evaporation of a liquid can be best carried out in a:
questions.solution: A China dish has a wide, shallow surface which maximises the surface area exposed to air, thereby accelerating evaporation. Greater surface area means more molecules at the surface can escape into the vapour phase. Common mistake: choosing a beaker (narrow opening restricts evaporation) over a China dish.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The state of matter which consists of super energetic particles in the form of ionized gases is called:
questions.solution: Plasma is the fourth state of matter consisting of super-energetic, ionised gas particles (free electrons and positive ions). It is found in stars, lightning, and neon signs. Common mistake: confusing plasma with the gaseous state — plasma differs in that particles are ionised.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following pair of gases **cannot** be separated by diffusion method?
questions.solution: Graham's Law of Diffusion: rate \(\propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\). \(CO_2\) has molar mass 44 g/mol and \(N_2O\) also has molar mass 44 g/mol. Since both have the same molar mass, they diffuse at the same rate and cannot be separated by diffusion. Common mistake: not checking molar masses carefully.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Sugar syrup, usually used to coat sweets with sugar, becomes hard when cooled. From this we can conclude that sugar syrup is:
questions.solution: When a saturated solution (maximum solute dissolved at a given temperature) is cooled, solubility decreases and excess solute crystallises out, causing hardening. This behaviour is characteristic of a saturated solution. Common mistake: choosing unsaturated — an unsaturated solution would simply remain liquid on cooling.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: To separate the solids which are insoluble in liquids such that solid is heavier than liquid:
questions.solution: When solid particles are heavier than the liquid and insoluble, they settle at the bottom by gravity (sedimentation). The clear liquid above is then carefully poured off (decantation). Common mistake: choosing filtration — filtration is used for mixtures where solid doesn't settle easily or must be collected.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which changes of state occur during distillation?
questions.solution: In distillation, the liquid mixture is first heated to boiling (liquid → vapour), and the vapour is then passed through a condenser where it cools and condenses back to liquid (vapour → liquid). Common mistake: confusing the order — boiling always precedes condensation in distillation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: When liquid starts boiling, further heat energy which is supplied:
questions.optionA: Is lost to the surroundings as much
questions.optionB: Increases the temperature of the liquid
questions.optionC: Increases the kinetic energy of the particles in the liquid
questions.optionD: Is absorbed as latent heat of vaporisation by the liquid.
questions.solution: At the boiling point, temperature remains constant despite continued heating. The supplied heat energy is used to overcome intermolecular forces and is absorbed as latent heat of vaporisation (\(L_v\)), converting liquid to vapour without temperature change. Common mistake: assuming temperature keeps rising at boiling point.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The forces of attraction between the particles of matter is maximum in:
questions.solution: Iron is a solid metal held together by strong metallic bonds (free electron sea model), which are among the strongest interparticle forces. Kerosene and glycerine are liquids with weaker intermolecular forces, and dry air is a gas with negligible forces. Common mistake: choosing glycerine due to its high viscosity, but viscosity is not the same as strength of interparticle forces in the solid state.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: You can separate a mixture of sand, salt and water by:
questions.solution: Sand is insoluble in water and is removed by filtration (sand stays on filter paper; salt water passes through). Then salt water is separated by distillation or evaporation to recover salt and pure water. Common mistake: choosing option B — decantation works only if particles settle, which is not guaranteed for fine sand.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The substance with the least inter-particle space is:
questions.solution: Copper is a solid metal with a closely packed crystal lattice structure. Solids have the least interparticle spaces compared to liquids (methanol, acetic acid) and gases (oxygen). Common mistake: confusing substances — all that matters is the state: solid < liquid < gas for interparticle spacing.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Large volume of Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) is available in small cylinders to us due to its property of:
questions.solution: Gases have large intermolecular spaces and negligible intermolecular forces, making them highly compressible. CNG is stored at high pressure in small cylinders using this property. Common mistake: choosing low density — although gases have low density, it is the high compressibility that allows storage in small cylinders.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Alcohol exists as a liquid at room temperature because:
questions.optionA: The intermolecular forces are strong enough to keep its particles bound to each other
questions.optionB: Its melting point is below room temperature
questions.solution: Alcohol is liquid at room temperature because: (a) it has significant hydrogen bonding (intermolecular forces) keeping particles together, and (b) its melting point is below room temperature (e.g., ethanol melts at about \(-114^{\circ}C\)) so it is not solid. Both conditions together explain the liquid state. Common mistake: choosing only (a) and ignoring the melting point condition.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following statement is **NOT** true?
questions.optionA: A mixture of water and milk can be separated by filtration.
questions.optionB: A mixture of powdered salt and sugar can be separated by fractional crystallisation.
questions.optionC: Loading is a process which involves alum.
questions.optionD: Salt from sea water is obtained by evaporation.
questions.solution: Water and milk form a colloidal/homogeneous mixture (milk is an emulsion of fat in water). Filtration separates solid particles from liquid, but since both water and milk are liquids, filtration cannot separate them — centrifugation is used instead. Options B, C, D are all correct. Common mistake: thinking filtration can separate any two liquids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a solid-in-gas mixture?
questions.solution: Smoke is a colloidal dispersion of fine solid particles (carbon/ash) in a gas (air), making it a solid-in-gas mixture (aerosol). Soil is solid-solid, moisture is liquid-in-gas (aerosol of water), and dew is condensed water droplets (liquid). Common mistake: choosing moisture — moisture involves liquid water droplets in air, not solid particles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Two miscible liquids having different boiling points can be separated by:
questions.solution: Fractional distillation exploits the difference in boiling points of miscible liquids. The mixture is heated and vapours are collected at different temperatures in a fractionating column. Simple evaporation cannot separate two miscible liquids. Common mistake: choosing simple distillation instead of fractional distillation for miscible liquids with close boiling points.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: In an experiment oxygen was added to hydrogen and heated. On burning a substance containing both oxygen and hydrogen water was formed. What is this substance?
questions.solution: Water (\(H_2O\)) is formed by the chemical combination of hydrogen and oxygen in a fixed ratio (2:1). A compound is a pure substance formed by chemical combination of two or more elements in a definite ratio, which can only be separated by chemical means. Common mistake: choosing mixture — in a mixture, components retain their properties; in water, hydrogen and oxygen have lost their individual properties.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following indicates the relative randomness of particles in the three states of matter?
questions.solution: Randomness (disorder) of particles increases as: Solid (fixed positions, least random) < Liquid (can move but constrained) < Gas (completely random, maximum disorder). This is also related to entropy: \(S_{solid} < S_{liquid} < S_{gas}\). Common mistake: reversing the order.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Rate of evaporation of an aqueous solution decreases with increase in:
questions.solution: High humidity means the air already contains a large amount of water vapour, reducing the concentration gradient between the liquid surface and the air above. This slows the rate of evaporation. Wind speed, temperature, and surface area all increase evaporation rate. Common mistake: choosing temperature — higher temperature increases evaporation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The state of matter which consists of super energetic particles in the form of ionized gases is called:
questions.solution: Plasma is the fourth state of matter comprising ionised gas particles with extremely high kinetic energy. It is distinct from ordinary gas. Examples include the sun, lightning, and fluorescent lamps. Common mistake: confusing plasma with the gaseous state.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The quantity of matter present in an object is called its:
questions.solution: Mass is defined as the quantity of matter present in an object and is a scalar quantity measured in kilograms (SI unit). Weight is the gravitational force acting on the mass (\(W = mg\)) and varies with gravity. Common mistake: confusing mass with weight — weight changes with location (gravity), mass does not.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Filtration as a method of separation can be used for mixtures that are:
questions.solution: Filtration is applicable to heterogeneous mixtures where an insoluble solid is suspended in a liquid (solid-in-liquid). The filter paper retains the solid while the liquid (filtrate) passes through. Common mistake: applying filtration to homogeneous (solutions) mixtures — dissolved solutes cannot be separated by filtration.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following mixtures will be the most difficult to separate?
questions.solution: Nitrogen and hydrogen are both gases with similar physical properties. Separating gaseous mixtures requires techniques like fractional distillation at very low temperatures (liquefaction), which is extremely difficult. The other mixtures involve physical differences (magnetic, density, solubility) easily exploited. Common mistake: thinking iron filings + sand is hardest — it is easily separated using a magnet.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: 450 K temperature may be written in Celsius scale as:
questions.solution: The conversion formula is: \(T(^{\circ}C) = T(K) - 273\). So \(450 - 273 = 177^{\circ}C\). Common mistake: adding 273 instead of subtracting (which would give \(723^{\circ}C\)) or using 273.15 which changes the answer slightly.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following states has the least energetic molecules?
questions.solution: In solids, molecules are held in fixed positions by strong interparticle forces with only vibrational motion — making them the least energetic. Energy order: Plasma > Gas > Liquid > Solid. Common mistake: none typically; this is straightforward but students may confuse potential and kinetic energy.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Which of the following process(es) release(s) heat?
1. Condensation
2. Vaporisation
3. Freezing
4. Melting
questions.solution: Condensation (gas→liquid) and freezing (liquid→solid) are exothermic processes — they release latent heat to the surroundings. Vaporisation and melting are endothermic (absorb heat). Common mistake: confusing exothermic/endothermic — whenever molecules come closer (condense/freeze), energy is released; when they move apart (vaporise/melt), energy is absorbed.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A liquid is kept in a China dish. The evaporation of the liquid can be accelerated:
questions.optionA: By keeping the dish in the open
questions.optionB: By blowing air into the liquid
questions.optionC: By keeping the dish under a running fan
questions.solution: All three methods increase evaporation rate: keeping in open increases surface area exposed; blowing air removes saturated vapour (reduces humidity above liquid); running fan increases air movement reducing vapour concentration above liquid. Common mistake: choosing only one option without recognising all three are valid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Some crushed ice is put in a test tube and warmed. The ice melts because its particles:
questions.optionC: Gain heat energy and become closer
questions.optionD: Gain heat energy and move away from their fixed positions
questions.solution: On heating, ice particles gain thermal energy (latent heat of fusion) which overcomes the rigid intermolecular forces holding them in fixed positions. Particles begin to move away from their fixed positions, breaking the crystal lattice structure and transitioning to liquid. Common mistake: choosing C (become closer) — when solid melts to liquid, particles actually move slightly farther apart.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which word describes the following change? \(I_2(s) \rightarrow I_2(g)\)
questions.solution: Sublimation is the direct conversion of a substance from solid state to gaseous state without passing through the liquid state. Iodine (\(I_2\)) is a classic example of a sublimable substance. Common mistake: choosing evaporation — evaporation is liquid to gas, not solid to gas.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Evaporation of a material takes place:
questions.solution: Evaporation is a surface phenomenon that occurs at temperatures below the boiling point at all temperatures, as long as the substance is in the liquid state. Boiling occurs throughout the liquid at the boiling point. Common mistake: confusing evaporation with boiling — evaporation can occur even at room temperature (e.g., water evaporating from a puddle).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which one of the following statements is **wrong** for gases?
questions.optionA: Gases do not have a definite shape and volume.
questions.optionB: Volume of the gas is equal to the volume of the container confining the gas.
questions.optionC: Confined gas exerts uniform pressure on the walls of container in all directions.
questions.optionD: Mass of the gas cannot be determined by weighing a container in which it is enclosed.
questions.solution: The mass of a gas CAN be determined by weighing the container before and after filling with gas — the difference gives the gas mass. Options A, B, and C are all correct properties of gases. Common mistake: thinking gas mass cannot be measured because it is invisible.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Addition of impurities to water:
questions.optionA: Decreases the freezing point of water
questions.optionB: Increases the boiling point of water
questions.optionC: Does not affect the freezing or boiling point of water
questions.solution: Addition of impurities (solutes) causes two colligative effects: (1) elevation of boiling point: \(\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m\), and (2) depression of freezing point: \(\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m\). Both effects occur simultaneously. Common mistake: choosing only one effect (A or B) and not recognising both occur together.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following represent the densest state of matter?
questions.solution: Solids have the highest density because their particles are most closely packed with minimum interparticle spaces. Density order: Solid > Liquid > Gas ≈ Vapour. Common mistake: some students confuse density with energy — gases have highest energy but lowest density.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: When ice melts to water, then heat is:
questions.solution: Melting (fusion) is an endothermic process. Ice absorbs latent heat of fusion (\(3.34 \times 10^5\) J/kg) from the surroundings to break the hydrogen bonds in the crystal lattice and transition to liquid water. Temperature remains constant at \(0^{\circ}C\) during melting. Common mistake: choosing 'evolved' — heat is absorbed, not released during melting.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The quantity of matter present in an object is called its:
questions.solution: Mass is the measure of the amount of matter in a body, measured in kilograms (SI). Weight (\(W = mg\)) is the gravitational force, volume is the space occupied, and density is mass per unit volume. Common mistake: confusing mass with weight.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Which of the following statements is/are correct?
(A) Intermolecular forces of attraction in solids are maximum.
(B) Intermolecular forces of attraction in gases are minimum.
(C) Intermolecular spaces in solids are minimum.
questions.optionA: Intermolecular forces of attraction in solids are maximum.
questions.optionB: Intermolecular forces of attraction in gases are minimum.
questions.optionC: Intermolecular spaces in solids are minimum.
questions.solution: All three statements are correct: Solids have maximum intermolecular forces (particles held in fixed positions), gases have minimum intermolecular forces (particles move freely), and solids have minimum interparticle spaces (closely packed). This follows from the standard comparison of the three states of matter.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What happens to the volume of the solution when small amount of sugar is dissolved in it?
questions.solution: When sugar dissolves in water, the total volume of the solution increases slightly compared to the volume of pure water alone, as the sugar molecules occupy some additional space in the solution. However, the increase is less than the volume of dry sugar because sugar molecules fit into the interstitial spaces. Common mistake: choosing 'no change' — the volume does increase, though not by the full volume of sugar.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is **not** an example of matter?
questions.solution: Matter is defined as anything that has mass and occupies space. Air (mixture of gases) and dust (solid particles) both have mass and volume, so they are matter. A 'feeling of cold' is a sensory perception — it has no mass and occupies no space, so it is not matter. Common mistake: choosing 'none of these' or air, which clearly has mass and volume.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct?
questions.optionA: Interparticle spaces are maximum in the gaseous state of a substance.
questions.optionB: Particles which constitute the matter follow a zig-zag path.
questions.optionC: Solid state is the most compact state of substance.
questions.solution: All three statements are correct: Gases have maximum interparticle spaces; particles in matter undergo Brownian/random motion (zig-zag path); solids are the most compact with minimum spacing. Common mistake: choosing only A, B, or C individually without recognising all three are simultaneously true.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which out of the following does not make sense?
questions.optionA: Solids have fixed shape and fixed volume.
questions.optionB: Liquids can be compressed easily, but not gases.
questions.optionC: The particles of solids have negligible kinetic energy.
questions.optionD: Property of diffusion is maximum in the gaseous state.
questions.solution: It is gases that are highly compressible (large interparticle spaces), while liquids are nearly incompressible (particles already close together). The statement 'liquids can be compressed easily, but not gases' is exactly the reverse of reality. Common mistake: choosing C — while solids have low kinetic energy, it is not exactly zero/negligible (they vibrate).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question:
Which of the following is/are application(s) of high compressibility of gases?
(A) LPG is used as fuel in homes for cooking food.
(B) Oxygen cylinders are supplied to hospitals.
(C) CNG is used as fuel in vehicles.
questions.optionA: LPG is used as fuel in homes for cooking food.
questions.optionB: Oxygen cylinders are supplied to hospitals.
questions.optionC: CNG is used as fuel in vehicles.
questions.solution: All three are applications of high compressibility of gases: LPG, oxygen, and CNG are all compressed gases stored in cylinders at high pressure. High compressibility allows large volumes of gas to be stored in small cylinders. Common mistake: thinking only CNG compressibility matters — all gaseous fuels and medical gases use this property.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following statements does **not** go with the liquid state?
questions.optionA: Particles are loosely packed in the liquid state.
questions.optionB: Fluidity is the maximum in the liquid state.
questions.optionD: Liquids take up the shape of any container in which these are placed.
questions.solution: Fluidity is maximum in the **gaseous** state (not liquid state) because gas particles move most freely with negligible intermolecular forces. Liquids do flow (have more fluidity than solids) but less than gases. Options A, C, D are correct for liquids. Common mistake: thinking liquids have maximum fluidity because they flow visibly.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: On changing which of the following, the states of matter can be changed?
questions.solution: Both temperature and pressure changes can alter the state of matter. Increasing temperature provides energy for phase transition (e.g., melting, boiling); changing pressure can also cause phase changes (e.g., pressurising a gas liquefies it, reducing pressure on water lowers its boiling point). Common mistake: choosing only temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Melting and freezing point of water:
questions.solution: The melting point of ice and the freezing point of water are the same: \(0^{\circ}C\) at standard pressure. At this temperature, the solid and liquid phases coexist in equilibrium. Common mistake: thinking melting point and freezing point are different temperatures — they are the same for a pure substance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Latent heat of vaporisation of water is:
questions.solution: The latent heat of vaporisation of water is \(2.26 \times 10^{6}\) J/kg \(= 22.5 \times 10^{5}\) J/kg (approximately). The latent heat of fusion of ice is \(3.34 \times 10^{5}\) J/kg (option C) — a common confusion. Common mistake: confusing latent heat of vaporisation with latent heat of fusion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: S.I. unit of temperature is:
questions.solution: The SI (International System of Units) unit of temperature is the Kelvin (K). Celsius (\(^{\circ}C\)) is a derived unit commonly used but not the SI base unit. Conversion: \(T(K) = T(^{\circ}C) + 273.15\). Common mistake: choosing Celsius as the SI unit because it is more commonly used in daily life.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: When a liquid starts boiling, the further heat energy which is supplied:
questions.optionA: Is lost to the surrounding as such.
questions.optionB: Increases the temperature of the liquid.
questions.optionC: Increases the kinetic energy of the liquid.
questions.optionD: Is absorbed as latent heat of vaporisation by the liquid.
questions.solution: At boiling point, temperature remains constant. Supplied heat is used as latent heat of vaporisation to overcome intermolecular forces and convert liquid to vapour. \(Q = mL_v\) where \(L_v\) is the specific latent heat of vaporisation. Common mistake: assuming temperature continues to rise during boiling.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Solids cannot be compressed because:
questions.optionA: Constituent particles are very closely packed.
questions.optionC: Movement of constituent particles is restricted.
questions.solution: Solids cannot be compressed because their constituent particles are already in close contact with very small interparticle spaces. There is virtually no room to push the particles closer together. Common mistake: choosing C (restricted movement) — restricted movement explains rigidity, not incompressibility; incompressibility is due to closely packed particles.
